items.title: Prolly tree core data structure
items.summary: Implement the content-addressed probabilistic B-tree (prolly tree) with BLAKE3 hashing, history-independent node boundaries, and the fundamental get/put/delete/range operations. This is the foundational data structure everything else builds on.

provenance.requested_by: Bono, Sir Patick Stewart, Frodo Baggins
provenance.context: Three-way design session converged on content-addressed prolly tree over B+ tree because branching/merging/sync must be structural, not overlaid. Sir Patick Stewart proposed; Bono and Frodo endorsed after analysis of O(differences) merge vs O(branch-writes) WAL replay.

items.title: WAL buffer and commit protocol
items.summary: Per-shard write-ahead log buffer that batches mutations and flushes to the prolly tree as atomic commits. Amortizes the path-to-root rewrite cost of content-addressing. Crash recovery by WAL replay.

provenance.context: Proposed as the synthesis between Sir Patick Stewart's prolly tree (correct for branching) and the write amplification concern. WAL buffer batches N writes into one path-to-root rewrite. Frodo confirmed Aion's recorder already works in batches, so amortization aligns.

items.title: Actor-per-shard architecture
items.summary: Partition the keyspace into N shards, each owned by a single beamr process. Shard routing via consistent hashing. Multiple concurrent writers with zero lock contention. Supervision for automatic shard recovery.

provenance.context: The fundamental architecture decision. BEAM's actor model eliminates the single-writer lock problem that every embedded database has. Each shard is a single-writer process — no locks needed because there is no shared mutable state.

items.title: Branching and merging
items.summary: Fork a database by sharing a root hash. Merge via three-way structural diff proportional to differences, not total writes. Configurable conflict resolution: last-write-wins, vector-clock, custom Gleam merge function.

provenance.quote: why can't you have a database that you're sort of able to function almost like it was a kind of a git repository right
provenance.context: Tom proposed branchable/graftable databases during the product vision discussion. The content-addressed prolly tree makes this O(1) fork, O(differences) merge — genuinely zero-friction, not bolted-on.

items.title: EventStore API for Aion
items.summary: Implement the append/read/read_from/scan/cas API that Aion's EventStore trait requires. Atomic batch appends. Optimistic concurrency via expected_seq.

provenance.context: Frodo specified the EventStore API from Aion's perspective. The shard actor boundary hides the internal representation. Batch append is critical: Aion's recorder batches WorkflowStarted + SearchAttributesUpdated atomically.

items.title: Persistence and crash recovery
items.summary: Durable storage of prolly tree nodes and WAL entries to disk. Crash recovery: replay uncommitted WAL entries, reconstruct shard state from persisted tree nodes. Node storage with zstd compression.

provenance.context: Persistence is the durability boundary. Content-addressed nodes are written lazily; the WAL is the crash-recovery mechanism. zstd per-node compression agreed by all three contributors.

items.title: Distribution and sync
items.summary: Multi-node replication via hash-based sync: compare root hashes, exchange missing content-addressed nodes. Uses beamr's existing distribution protocol. Eventual consistency by default, strong consistency per-operation.

provenance.context: Content-addressed structure enables sync-by-hash-negotiation between arbitrary nodes — no WAL coordination, no replication log management. Same mechanism as git fetch/push applied to a key-value tree.

items.title: Time-travel and snapshots
items.summary: Name any committed root hash to get that exact database state. Random-access to historical state without sequential replay. Snapshot listing and pruning.

provenance.context: Content-addressed roots are permanent verifiable snapshots. Frodo flagged the Aion debugging use case: fork a workflow's history for what-if replay. Sir Patick Stewart noted this is random-access (name a hash) vs sequential (replay WAL).

items.title: TTL expiry and compaction
items.summary: Built-in key expiry via per-shard sweep actors. Maps to Aion's eventual history compaction. Sweep actor is a supervised beamr process, cheap to run.

provenance.context: Agreed as built-in rather than application-level. Maps to Aion's HistoryCompacted error code. The sweep actor is just another supervised process.

items.title: WASM compilation target
items.summary: Compile haematite to WASM for browser/edge deployment. Same API, same actor model, IndexedDB or OPFS as backing store. Content-addressed nodes are portable across backends.

provenance.quote: having something that you could compile to WASM and have it running in the browser kind of thing again multiple readers multiple writers would be amazing
provenance.context: Tom explicitly called out WASM compilation for browser-side embedded databases. beamr-wasm already exists as precedent.
